title: Chapter 7: The First Fire
content: The conservatory smelled of dead orchids and candle smoke.

We'd been at it for twenty minutes — circling, snapping, our voices climbing the glass walls and falling back like something alive. The curved ceiling caught our echoes and threw them at us from odd angles, so sometimes Heath's words hit me from behind, from above, from the ghost-lit corners where panes had shattered and let in the November cold.
"Waiting is a luxury we don't have," he said again, and I could hear the strain in it, the wire pulled tight behind his teeth.

"The Linton visit is in nine days. Nine days, Heath. We wait, we watch, we gather enough to bury Sloane so deep he never climbs out. You run your mouth now, and we're just two students making accusations against the headmaster. You know how that ends."
"You're afraid."
"I'm *strategic*."
"You're hiding behind patience because you won't risk anything."

He was close. Too close. Somewhere in the argument we'd stopped standing on opposite sides of the stone planter and started drifting toward each other like iron filings to a magnet. I could feel the heat of him — not metaphorical, actual body heat radiating off his chest, which was heaving from the force of his own argument. His jaw was set so hard I could see the tendon jumping.
"Risk," I spat. "You think I won't risk anything? I've risked everything since the moment I walked into this arrangement."
"Then why won't you—"
"Because *rage* isn't a plan, Heath. Rage is what Sloane counts on. Rage is what he swallows whole and spits back at you when you're too spent to defend yourself."

His hands came up — not at me, at the air, at the useless futility of arguing with someone who matched him word for word. "So what do you want? To wait forever? To be the good little student who follows the rules until Sloane decides it's time for the next—"
I slapped my palm flat against his chest to shut him up.
The contact point burned.

His hand came up and closed around my wrist. Not pulling. Holding. His grip was hard enough that I felt my pulse thudding against his fingers, and I knew — I *knew* — he could feel it too. That my body was betraying every measured word I'd just said.

We stood there, breathing. His breath harsh and ragged in my ear, mine coming in short pulls that made my ribs ache. The candle on the planter guttered between us, throwing our shadows up against the glass, two distorted giants frozen mid-conflict.
I should have stepped back. He should have let go.
Neither of us did.
"This isn't about the plan anymore," I said, and my voice came out lower than I intended, scraped raw.
"No," he said. "It isn't."
And then I was pulling him toward me by the collar of his shirt — that damn uniform shirt I'd imagined tearing off him in dark rooms I'd never admit to — and his mouth hit mine like a collision.

It wasn't a kiss. A kiss implies something soft, something tentative, a question asked with lips. This was a claim staked with teeth. His mouth was hot and hard and unapologetic, and I bit his lower lip hard enough that I tasted copper — bright and metallic and *real* — flooding across my tongue. He made a sound against my mouth, something between a growl and a gasp, and his hands found my hips with a grip that would leave marks.
Good.

I fisted my hands in his hair and pulled. His head jerked back and the sound he made then — low, broken, *surrendering* — was the most honest thing I'd ever heard from him. His throat was exposed, the long line of it candlelit and vulnerable, and I wanted to put my teeth there. I wanted to mark him the way he was marking me, bruise for bruise, claim for claim.

He lifted me like I weighed nothing. My back hit the planter's stone edge and then I was sitting on it, my legs wrapped around him, my fingers still twisted in his hair hard enough to hurt. The stone was cold through my skirt but I didn't feel it — all I could feel was the heat of him, the press of his body, the way his hands were rough on my waist, my thighs, my hips, pulling me against him like he was trying to fuse us together through sheer force of will.
"Gentle—" he started, some last vestige of conditioning making him hesitate, and I yanked his hair harder.
"Don't you dare be gentle with me."

The words came out like a command and a confession and a dare, all at once. I watched something break behind his eyes — the last chain, the last restraint, whatever had been holding him in the shape of a boy who followed rules and kept his hands to himself.
He wasn't gentle.

His mouth found my neck and I arched into it, my spine a bow, my breath coming in sharp bursts that echoed off the glass ceiling. His teeth were on my collarbone, my jaw, the soft place beneath my ear, and every point of contact was a small violence that I met with my own. I clawed down his back. I felt the fabric of his shirt tear under my nails, the rough give of cotton stitching separating, the warmer skin beneath. He was marks and heat and mine — *mine* — and I was taking what I wanted for the first time in a life that had taught me wanting was weakness.

We were two weapons test-firing on each other. Every bruise he left on my hip was a language I read fluently. Every scratch I raked down his shoulder blade was an answer in the same dialect. The conservatory was a furnace of our making — the candlelight caught the shattered glass overhead and scattered broken stars across the stone floor, and somewhere below all of it the acid-sweet smell of the orchids' decay mixed with candle smoke and sweat and the copper still on my tongue, and the only sounds were breath and the whisper of torn fabric and the small, devastating noises two people make when they forget they're supposed to be armor instead of flesh.
I don't know how long it lasted. Time doesn't work in furnaces.

What I know is this: at some point his forehead dropped to my shoulder, and my hands loosened in his hair, and the storm didn't stop so much as exhaust itself. We sat there, his weight against me, my fingers still curved around strands of his hair but no longer pulling. The candle had burned down to a stub, its flame a weak blue thing, and the smoke from the guttering wick curled between us like a question neither of us was brave enough to ask.

I slid off the planter. My legs were unsteady. I made it three feet before I sat down hard on the stone floor, my back against the cold wall, and I stayed there because standing seemed like a performance I couldn't manage yet.
Heath sat down across from me. Not next to me. Across. Like a negotiation table for two people who had just detonated something they couldn't un-detonate.
The silence wasn't comfortable. It wasn't awkward either. It was the silence after an explosion, before the dust settles enough to see what's still standing.

I could feel the bruises blooming on my hips. My lips were swollen. When I touched my lower lip with my tongue, I tasted blood again — his teeth had caught me somewhere along the way, a reverse echo of where I'd caught him. My hair was a ruin; I could feel it tangled and wild against my neck, strands stuck to the sweat at my temples.

Heath's shirt was torn from the collar to the left shoulder. The fabric hung open, revealing three parallel scratches I'd carved down his chest, already raising into red welts. His hair looked like something had tried to pull it out — because something had. His knuckles, resting on his knee, were white.
We didn't look at each other.

The candle died. Smoke rose from the blackened wick in a thin, straight line, and in the sudden darkness the broken ceiling became a map of stars — the real stars, cold and indifferent, visible through the gaps where glass had given up and shattered outward.
"That wasn't love," I said.
My voice was steady. I was proud of that.
"No," Heath said, equally steady. "That was necessity."
The word hung in the dark between us. Necessity. Like air. Like water. Like something you die without.
I almost laughed. He almost laughed. Neither of us did.

We sat in the dark a while longer, and I listened to our breathing slow — his first, then mine, our ribs finding separate rhythms again after whatever synchronization had seized them. The stone under me was slick with cold. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groaned. The world outside the conservatory existed. I'd forgotten about it entirely.

When I finally moved, my body protested — muscles I hadn't used in that way, or ever, making themselves known. I stood. I found the spare hair pins on the planter where they'd fallen. I started fixing my hair, pulling it back into something that wouldn't get me stopped in the corridor, and my hands knew the motions even when my mind was elsewhere.

Heath stood too. He turned away from me and started buttoning his shirt, working around the tear, his movements methodical and precise. The transformation was surgical: the wild-eyed boy with the torn shirt and the bitten lip was being folded up and stored somewhere internal, and in his place the Heath Cruz of Blackwood Academy was being assembled — composed, unreadable, untouchable.

I was doing the same thing. Smoothing the wrinkled fabric of my skirt. Checking that my collar covered the mark on my neck — it didn't quite, so I adjusted the angle. Pinning my hair into submission. The mask going back on, piece by piece, as rehearsed as a costume change before the second act.
But I caught something.

The broken glass of the ceiling — a large curved shard still wedged in its frame — acted as a warped mirror. In it, I could see Heath's reflection. He thought I couldn't. He was watching me fix my collar, his hands stalled on a button halfway up his chest, and his face—
His face was not composed. His face was not unreadable.
It was terrified.

Not of me. I knew what fear of me looked like — I'd seen it in the eyes of girls who crossed my path, boys who underestimated me. This was different. This was the terror of a man who has just discovered a need inside himself so large it could eat him alive. A need he hadn't known was there until someone came along and set it free.

He needed what had just happened. Not wanted. *Needed*. The way you need air. The way you need the next breath. And that need terrified him more than anything Sloane could do to us, because Sloane was an enemy he could fight, but this — this was a weakness carved into his own chest, and he didn't know how to arm himself against it.
I looked away before he caught me watching.
"Right," I said, and my voice was the correct voice, the Cat Vance voice, cool and controlled. "We should go. Separate exits."
He nodded. He finished buttoning his shirt. The scratches were hidden now, pressed under cotton, but I knew they were there. I would always know they were there.
I turned toward the east corridor door. He turned toward the west. The script we'd memorized. The pattern. The way we'd always done it.
But he stopped.
At the door, his hand on the frame, his back to me, the line of his shoulders rigid beneath the torn shirt. He didn't turn around.
"This changes nothing."
The words landed between us like stones dropped into still water. Ripples spreading outward, distorting everything they touched.
"I know," I said.

I left first this time. Down the east corridor, my footsteps measured and quiet on the stone, my hand trailing along the wall to guide me through the dark. The mark on my neck throbbed in time with my pulse. My hips ached where his hands had gripped. My lip was still swollen, and when I bit it gently I tasted that copper again — his blood or mine, I couldn't tell anymore.

The entrance hall was empty. The green flame in its iron sconce — the one that never went out, the one that Sloane kept burning as a symbol of whatever dark genesis this academy was built on — caught my eye as I crossed toward the stairs.

It was burning higher than usual. The green fire licked up past the rim of the sconce, reaching toward the ceiling like fingers grasping for something just out of reach. The light it cast was wrong — too bright, too hungry, throwing my shadow long and distorted across the marble floor.

I stopped. I watched it for a moment, that green fire, and something in its reach mirrored something in my chest. A heat that wouldn't stay contained. A flame that had found oxygen and was devouring it.
This changes nothing, he'd said.
I know, I'd said.

The green fire surged again, climbing higher, and I turned away from it and climbed the stairs to my room, and I did not think about the terror in his reflection, and I did not think about the bruise his hands had left on my hip, and I did not think about how alive I had felt with his hair between my fingers and his surrender in my mouth.
I did not think about any of it.
The fire between us was lit. It would not go out.
And the worst part — the part I would never say aloud, not even to myself in the deepest watch of the night — was that I didn't want it to.
